---
title: "Homework 6: Bayesian Foundations, Time Series, and Hierarchical Models"
subtitle: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting — University of Nebraska at Omaha"
author: "Your Name"
date: "Spring 2026"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    self-contained: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Overview

Bayesian inference treats model parameters as random variables with probability
distributions. Instead of a single point estimate, you get a full posterior
distribution over every parameter — and over every forecast. This matters for
business: you can ask "What is the probability that next month's sales exceed our
budget target?" and get a direct, calibrated answer.

This homework has three parts. **Part A** (Lecture 10) builds a Beta-Binomial model
for promotion lift. **Part B** (Lecture 11) fits a Bayesian structural time series
model to Store 1. **Part C** (Lecture 11) extends to a hierarchical model across
all 30 stores, demonstrating partial pooling.

**Learning objectives:**

- Specify priors, likelihood, and posterior in PyMC
- Run prior predictive checks before fitting
- Fit a Bayesian trend + Fourier seasonality model to weekly sales
- Build a hierarchical model with store-level partial pooling
- Visualize and interpret the shrinkage effect of partial pooling

---

## Dataset and Setup

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pymc as pm
import arviz as az
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

RANDOM_STATE = 42

weekly = pd.read_csv("data/processed/rossmann_weekly.csv", parse_dates=["WeekStart"])

# Store 1 for Parts A and B
store1 = weekly[weekly["Store"] == 1].sort_values("WeekStart").reset_index(drop=True)

# All 30 stores for Part C
all_stores = weekly.sort_values(["Store", "WeekStart"]).reset_index(drop=True)

print(f"Store 1: {len(store1)} weekly observations")
print(f"All stores: {len(all_stores)} rows, {all_stores['Store'].nunique()} stores")
```

---

## Initial Prompt for Codex

::: {.callout-important}
## Initial Prompt for Codex

Paste the following as your first message to Codex. Save it as `INITIAL_PROMPT.md`
in your submission folder.

```
I am working on Homework 7 for BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting at the
University of Nebraska at Omaha.

Dataset: Rossmann Store Sales — weekly aggregated at
`data/processed/rossmann_weekly.csv`.
- Part A + B use Store 1 only (weekly Sales and Promo columns).
- Part C uses all 30 stores.

This homework covers Bayesian inference using PyMC (v4+):
  - Part A: Beta-Binomial model for promotion lift
  - Part B: Bayesian structural time series (trend + Fourier seasonality)
  - Part C: Hierarchical partial pooling model across 30 stores

I will ask you questions one at a time. For each:
1. Write clean, well-commented PyMC code that runs end-to-end
2. Interpret posterior distributions in plain English for a business audience
3. Log this interaction to PROMPT_LOG.jsonl before responding

My prompt budget is 15 (this context prompt is excluded). I will label
each question before asking.

Confirm by responding with: HW06_CONTEXT_ACK
```
:::

---

## Part A: Bayesian Foundations — Promotion Lift

### A1 — Prior Predictive Check *(Suggested prompts: 2)*

We want to estimate the weekly promotion rate — the probability that any given week
runs a promotion — and see how that rate varies. Model the number of promotion weeks
out of the total using a Beta-Binomial model:

- Prior: `theta ~ Beta(2, 2)` (weakly informative; centered at 0.5)
- Likelihood: `obs ~ Binomial(n=len(store1), p=theta)`
- Observed: total number of promotion weeks in Store 1's history

**Before fitting:** run `pm.sample_prior_predictive(500)` and plot the prior
predictive distribution. Does it generate plausible values for the number of
promotion weeks?

```{python}
n_total = len(store1)
n_promo = store1["Promo"].sum()
print(f"Store 1: {n_promo} promotion weeks out of {n_total} ({100*n_promo/n_total:.1f}%)")

# Build and prior-predictive-check the Beta-Binomial model
```

> **Your response:** What range of values does the prior predictive distribution
> cover? Is the prior too wide, too narrow, or appropriate? How would you tighten
> it if you already knew promotions run roughly 40% of weeks?

---

### A2 — Posterior Inference *(Suggested prompts: 2)*

Fit the Beta-Binomial model from A1 using `pm.sample(2000)`.
Report:

- Posterior mean and 94% HDI for `theta`
- `P(theta > 0.5)` — probability that promotions run more than half the time
- Plot the posterior distribution with the HDI shaded

```{python}
# Sample and summarize the posterior
```

> **Your response:** Interpret the posterior in one sentence suitable for a
> non-technical manager. What does the HDI tell you that a frequentist
> confidence interval cannot?

---

## Part B: Bayesian Time Series — Store 1

### B1 — Bayesian Trend + Fourier Seasonality *(Suggested prompts: 3)*

Fit a Bayesian structural time series model for Store 1 weekly sales with:

- Linear trend: `alpha ~ Normal(y.mean(), y.std())`, `beta ~ Normal(0, 1)`
- Fourier seasonality with K=3 harmonics and period P=52:
  `a_k, b_k ~ Normal(0, sigma_s)` for k=1,2,3
- Observation noise: `sigma ~ HalfNormal(y.std())`
- Likelihood: `Sales ~ Normal(mu, sigma)`

Use the first 100 weeks as training; the remaining weeks as test.
Sample with `pm.sample(1000, tune=1000)`.

```{python}
y = store1["Sales"].values
t = np.arange(len(y))
y_train, y_test = y[:100], y[100:]
t_train, t_test = t[:100], t[100:]
K, P = 3, 52

# Build and sample the Bayesian TS model
```

> **Your response:** What is the posterior mean and 94% HDI for the slope `beta`?
> Is the trend increasing or decreasing? With what probability?

---

### B2 — Posterior Predictive Forecast *(Suggested prompts: 2)*

Generate posterior predictive samples for the test period using
`pm.sample_posterior_predictive`. Plot:

1. Training data
2. Test data (actual)
3. Posterior predictive median + 90% credible band for the test period

Also compute test RMSE using the posterior predictive median. Compare to the
best classical model from Homework 1.

```{python}
# Generate and plot posterior predictive forecast
```

> **Your response:** How wide are the 90% credible bands? Does the actual test
> data fall within them? What does a wide band tell a demand planner compared to
> a narrow band?

---

## Part C: Hierarchical Model — 30 Stores

### C1 — Partial Pooling Model *(Suggested prompts: 3)*

Fit a hierarchical model for all 30 stores simultaneously. Each store gets its
own intercept `alpha_i`, but all intercepts are drawn from a shared Normal distribution:

```
mu_alpha ~ Normal(0, 1)              # group mean (standardized)
sigma_alpha ~ HalfNormal(1)          # group spread
alpha_i ~ Normal(mu_alpha, sigma_alpha)   # store intercepts (shape=30)
beta ~ Normal(0, 1)                  # shared weekly trend
sigma ~ HalfNormal(1)                # observation noise
Sales_it ~ Normal(alpha_i + beta * t, sigma)
```

Standardize Sales and the time index before fitting. Use `pm.sample(1000, tune=1000)`.

```{python}
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Standardize
scaler = StandardScaler()
all_stores["Sales_std"] = scaler.fit_transform(all_stores[["Sales"]])
all_stores["t_std"] = (
    all_stores.groupby("Store")["WeekStart"]
    .transform(lambda x: (x - x.min()).dt.days / 7)
)
all_stores["t_std"] = (all_stores["t_std"] - all_stores["t_std"].mean()) / all_stores["t_std"].std()

store_idx = all_stores["Store"].values - 1   # 0-indexed
J = all_stores["Store"].nunique()

# Build and sample the hierarchical model
```

> **Your response:** What are the posterior mean and 94% HDI for `sigma_alpha`?
> A large `sigma_alpha` means stores differ a lot from each other.
> A small value means they are similar. What does your posterior suggest?

---

### C2 — Shrinkage Plot *(Suggested prompts: 2)*

The key result of partial pooling is **shrinkage**: stores with fewer observations
get pulled toward the group mean; stores with more data retain their own estimate.

Compute for each store:
- The no-pooling estimate: store mean of standardized Sales
- The partial-pooling estimate: posterior mean of `alpha_i`

Plot these as two connected points per store (a caterpillar plot), sorted by
no-pooling estimate. Highlight the 5 stores with the fewest observations.

```{python}
# Shrinkage plot: no-pooling vs partial-pooling store intercepts
```

> **Your response:** Do the 5 stores with the fewest observations show more
> shrinkage than data-rich stores? Explain in one sentence why partial pooling is
> preferable to fitting 30 independent models for sparse stores.

---

### C3 — Pooling Comparison *(Suggested prompts: 2)*

Compare test RMSE for three pooling strategies on a held-out last-13-weeks split
for each store:

| Strategy | Description | Mean Test RMSE |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| Complete pooling | One intercept for all stores | |
| No pooling | 30 independent intercepts (separate fits) | |
| Partial pooling | Hierarchical model from C1 | |

For the complete and no-pooling models, use simple OLS (statsmodels or sklearn).
For partial pooling, use the posterior predictive mean from C1.

```{python}
# Fit complete pooling and no-pooling baselines; compare RMSE
```

> **Your response:** Which strategy gives the lowest mean test RMSE? Is the
> difference between partial and no pooling larger for stores with few
> observations or for stores with many? Why?

---

### Q_Final — Reflection *(Suggested prompts: 0 — write yourself)*

No code required. In 150–200 words: Rossmann has 1,115 stores. You have only
modeled 30. Explain how the hierarchical model would scale to all 1,115 stores —
specifically, what happens to the group-level estimates `mu_alpha` and `sigma_alpha`
as more stores are added. Then describe one business decision at Rossmann
(inventory, staffing, promotion scheduling) that would be improved by having a
well-calibrated posterior distribution over future sales rather than just a
point forecast.

> **Your response:**

---

## Submission Checklist

- [ ] All code cells run without errors from top to bottom
- [ ] Written responses completed in your own words
- [ ] `INITIAL_PROMPT.md` saved in `submissions/hw06_<your_name>/`
- [ ] `PROMPT_LOG.jsonl` complete with ≤ 15 entries
- [ ] One git commit per Codex prompt (`hw06 prompt <id>: <short summary>`)
- [ ] `REPORT.md` with a clear business recommendation
